title: Tool Use — Single Tool (Bash)
description: The agent uses a single tool (bash) to perform file system operations. This demonstrates the tool-call/tool-result cycle that extends the agent beyond pure text generation.

steps.content: Create a Hello.java file that prints 'Hello, Agent!' and compile it.
steps.annotation: The user requests a task that requires interacting with the file system. The LLM must decide to use a tool rather than just generating text.

steps.content: I'll create the Java file, compile it, and verify it works.
steps.annotation: The assistant acknowledges the plan before making tool calls. This is a brief planning step.
First tool call: the agent writes the Java source file using a heredoc in bash. The LLM chooses the tool and constructs the arguments — this is the 'Action' in the ReAct loop.
The tool returns its result. The agent observes the output to decide the next step — this is the 'Observation' in the ReAct loop.
The agent verifies the file was written correctly by reading it back. Good agents validate their own actions.
File contents confirmed. The observation matches expectations, so the agent proceeds to compilation.
Compile and run in one command. The '&&' ensures we only run if compilation succeeds.
The program compiled and ran successfully. The tool-call/tool-result cycle is complete.

steps.content:
Done! I created `Hello.java`, compiled it with `javac`, and ran it. The output is:

```
Hello, Agent!
```

The file is ready in your current directory.
steps.annotation: The agent summarizes what it did. The chat loop has completed one full iteration: user request → plan → tool calls → final response.
